questions.question: Which code has the highest circuit-level error threshold?

options.text: All codes have the same threshold

questions.explanation: The surface code has the highest circuit-level error threshold at approximately 0.8-1.1%. Color codes have thresholds around 0.1%, and concatenated codes are typically around 0.01%. Higher threshold means more tolerance for realistic hardware errors.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of color codes over surface codes?

options.text: Transversal implementation of all Clifford gates

questions.explanation: Color codes support transversal implementation of all Clifford gates (H, S, CNOT), while surface codes only support transversal CNOT. This makes Clifford operations much faster in color codes but comes at the cost of lower error threshold.

questions.question: When is a color code a better choice than a surface code?

options.text: When physical error rates are around 1%
When physical error rates are very low (<0.1%) and Clifford gates dominate
When the algorithm is T-gate heavy

questions.explanation: Color codes are advantageous when hardware is very clean (error rate <0.1%) and the algorithm is Clifford-heavy. The lower threshold requires better hardware, but the transversal Clifford gates provide significant speedup for Clifford-dominated circuits.

questions.question: How many logical qubits does the toric code encode per patch?
questions.explanation: The toric code encodes 2 logical qubits per patch due to the genus-1 topology of the torus (k = 2g = 2). This is double what a planar surface code encodes, but requires periodic boundary conditions.

questions.question: Why do surface codes dominate current experimental implementations?

options.text: They are the only codes that work
Highest threshold, 2D local connectivity, simple decoding

questions.explanation: Surface codes dominate because they have the highest practical threshold (~1%), require only 2D nearest-neighbor connectivity (matching most hardware), and have fast, well-understood MWPM decoders. This combination makes them the most practical choice for current devices.

questions.question: For an algorithm dominated by T gates, which code is typically preferable?
questions.explanation: For T-gate heavy algorithms, the surface code is preferable because its higher threshold provides better magic state fidelity. Both codes require magic state injection for T gates, but surface codes produce cleaner magic states due to their higher threshold.

questions.question: QLDPC and Floquet codes are emerging alternatives that may eventually surpass surface codes for some applications.
questions.explanation: True. QLDPC codes offer better asymptotic scaling (constant rate with linear distance), and Floquet codes use lower-weight measurements. These emerging codes are active research areas that may provide advantages for specific hardware or applications in the future.
